does: This paper identifies and mechanistically analyzes spatial lexical bias in multimodal large language models—a failure mode where adding a spatial relation word to answer options causes models to select that option even when they previously answered the question correctly. The authors test nine open-weight MLLMs and isolate diagnostic cases where models answer binary spatial questions correctly but fail on ternary versions, demonstrating the phenomenon is widespread. Using mechanistic interpretability tools including visual attention analysis, residual-stream probes, activation patching, and sparse component analysis, they demonstrate that the correct spatial relation remains internally available in the model's visual representations during these failures, placing responsibility for the error on language-side processing rather than visual perception.
connects: This work extends recent research on spatial reasoning failures in vision-language models, building on prior observations that MLLMs struggle with spatial understanding. It connects to broader lines of work in mechanistic interpretability applied to multimodal systems, following approaches that decompose model failures into constituent components. The paper responds to the common attribution of spatial failures to visual inattention or weak visual grounding by providing evidence of a complementary language-side bias, drawing on techniques from interpretability research that probe internal representations and causal mechanisms within transformer models. It relates to work on systematic biases in language models and their interaction with multimodal inputs.
enables: This work enables more targeted debugging of MLLM spatial reasoning by distinguishing visual from language-side failure modes, allowing future research to develop interventions addressing the specific mechanisms identified. The diagnostic methodology of identifying binary-stable but ternary-fragile cases could generalize to other domains beyond spatial reasoning where model decisions are fragile to option set composition. The findings point toward architectural or training modifications that reduce lexical bias while preserving spatial understanding. Understanding these mechanistic details supports development of more reliable MLLMs for embodied AI applications, robotic navigation, and spatial question-answering systems that require consistent spatial reasoning regardless of how options are presented.
